mappings.rationaleAI: Maxwell's unification of electricity, magnetism, and light (A.2.d.ii), kinetic theory of gases (A.2.d.iii), exemplifying physics' concern with matter and energy (C.1, C.2).

Alchemy as the precursor to modern chemistry is foundational to the history of the physical sciences (A.1 development of the physical sciences).

Franklin's electricity experiments, including the kite-in-a-thunderstorm, and his scientific inventions (bifocals, Franklin stove) represent significant contributions to the history of the physical sciences (A).

The discovery of dark energy belongs to the history of 20th-century astronomy, specifically relativistic cosmologies and open/closed universe models (A.1.f.iv) and identification of cosmic background radiation and related phenomena (A.1.f.vii).

Her work disseminating Newtonian mechanics belongs to the history of 16th-17th century physics including discoveries and theories in mechanics (A.2.c), which she translated and interpreted for the French-speaking world.
Noether's Theorem is regarded as one of the most important mathematical theorems influencing modern physics, connecting symmetry and conservation laws in 20th-century physics (A.2.e).

Development of quantum theory, wave mechanics, and statistical mechanics (A.2.e.ii) is central to the episode; philosophical problems in physics at the quantum level (C.6) directly relate to the measurement problem and interpretations of quantum mechanics.

The astronomical work of Brahe and Kepler at Rudolf's court relates to 16th-17th century astronomy (A.1.c) including the development of laws of planetary motion and use of telescopes; the alchemical research connects to chemistry before 1700 (A.3.a).
The intersection of astronomy and astrology during the Renaissance connects to 16th-17th century astronomy (A.1.c) and the evolution of physics and natural philosophy (A.2.c).

William Herschel's discovery of Uranus, detection of infrared radiation, and the Herschels' construction of powerful telescopes represent major advances in the physical sciences (A, B) - specifically observational astronomy and the physics of radiation
